topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official-looking document lay spread across the workbench, the embossed seal mocking me from its pristine white page. My hands, usually steady when calibrating delicate circuitry, were trembling so violently I had to grip the edge of the steel table to keep from dropping them. "Impossible," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the echoing workshop. I reread the section detailing the application number, the date, the specific nomenclature of my device. It was *mine*. My sleepless nights, the countless failed prototypes, the sheer, agonizing brilliance of the concept—all reduced to a bureaucratic stamp held by some other soul. A hot flush crept up my neck, turning my skin tight and prickly. I pushed the papers away with a sharp, jerky motion, the sound of the thick paper sliding across the wood sounding deafeningly loud. I needed air. I needed distance. My breath hitched in my chest, shallow and rapid, as I backed away from the desk, stumbling slightly until the cool metal of the shelving unit pressed against my spine.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the digital confirmation glowing an infuriating shade of green. Patent granted. To someone else. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. A strange, buzzing pressure built behind my eyes, a frantic, internal static that made the fluorescent lights above me seem to strobe. I started pacing the small confines of my studio, my movements becoming jerky, almost uncontrolled. "No. No, this isn't right," I muttered, running a hand through my already disheveled hair, feeling the strands stand up on my scalp. Every logical pathway I had constructed for this situation had evaporated. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges. I stopped abruptly, clutching my temples as a wave of sheer, overwhelming disbelief washed over me, leaving me breathless and shaky. I looked around the room, suddenly seeing every tool, every schematic, as evidence of a cruel, cosmic joke.

[story 3]

The phone call had been the final straw. The voice on the other end, smooth and utterly devoid of inflection, had simply recited the patent number. It was a flat statement of fact, and it shattered the fragile scaffolding of my self-belief. I dropped the receiver onto the floor; it made a dull, pathetic thud. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, a fine tremor that spread from my fingertips up my arms, making them feel alien and heavy. I stood rigid by the window, watching the street traffic flow below, unable to focus on anything. My heart hammered against my ribs with such force that I felt dizzy, a sickening lurch in my stomach. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the images—the blueprints, the late nights fueled by caffeine and pure genius—back into focus, but they dissolved into a swirling, chaotic mess. A strange, high-pitched ringing filled my ears, and I had to press the heels of my palms against my eyes, fighting the sudden, overwhelming urge to simply collapse onto the cold floor.
